3.2 Pressure in fluids at rest>Pascal's principle
3.2 Pressure in fluids at rest>Hydraulic press

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What characterizes steady (laminar) fluid flow?

options.A: Particles follow smooth, non-crossing paths with constant velocity at each point
options.B: Flow contains small whirlpool-like regions and random particle motion
options.C: Fluid moves with extremely high velocity and chaotic pressure changes
options.D: The flow occurs only in gases, not in liquids

questions.hint: Think about orderly vs chaotic flow patterns.
questions.explanation: Steady flow has smooth streamlines that never cross; each particle arriving at a point has the same velocity. Option B describes turbulent flow; Option D is false — liquids can also have laminar flow.

source_trace.content_anchor: steady fluid flow if each particle follows smooth path such that paths never cross

explanations_tiered.definition: Steady (laminar) flow is a smooth, orderly flow pattern where fluid particles move in parallel layers without crossing.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In laminar flow, each particle follows a streamline; velocity at any fixed point remains constant over time. Low velocities and small diameters promote laminar flow.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine cars on a highway all moving smoothly in their own lanes without changing lanes — that's laminar flow. No crossing, no chaos.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Laminar flow is characterized by parallel streamlines that do not intersect. The Reynolds number is low (\(Re < 2000\)), indicating viscous forces dominate.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In laminar flow, \(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} = 0\) at each point. The flow is deterministic and can be described by the Navier-Stokes equations without turbulence terms.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of steady flow from fluid dynamics.
Identify that streamlines do not cross.
Note constant velocity at each point over time.
misconceptions: Steady flow means constant speed (it means constant velocity at each point, but speed can vary along path).
Laminar flow only in liquids (it occurs in any fluid at low enough velocity).

glossary_entry.definition: Smooth, orderly fluid flow in parallel layers without mixing.

flashcard.back: Smooth flow with non-crossing paths and constant velocity at each point over time.

visual_system.visual_description: Left: parallel smooth streamlines (laminar). Right: chaotic, swirling paths (turbulent).
visual_system.caption: Laminar flow has smooth streamlines; turbulent flow has eddies and mixing.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Comparison diagram: laminar shows parallel lines; turbulent shows crossing swirls.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Smooth, non-crossing paths; constant velocity at each point over time.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Smooth fluid flow in parallel layers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use dye injection in water flow to demonstrate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate steady and turbulent fluid flow
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Steady flow has smooth, non-crossing streamlines with constant velocity at each point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How does the buoyant force on a fully submerged object compare when the object is moved from fresh water to salt water (higher density)?

options.A: It decreases because salt water is denser
options.B: It increases because salt water is denser
options.C: It remains the same because the object's volume is unchanged
options.D: It becomes zero in salt water

questions.hint: Buoyant force depends on fluid density and displaced volume.
questions.explanation: \(F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}\). Salt water has higher density than fresh water, and \(V_{\text{disp}}\) is the same (fully submerged), so buoyant force increases. Option A is opposite; Option C ignores density change.

explanations_tiered.definition: Buoyant force equals the weight of fluid displaced: \(F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a fully submerged object, displaced volume equals object volume. Thus \(F_B \propto \rho_{\text{fluid}}\). Salt water is denser, so buoyant force increases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Salt water is "heavier" than fresh water. A submerged object pushes aside more weight of salt water, so it gets a bigger upward push.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(V_{\text{disp}} = V_{\text{obj}}\) constant, \(F_B\) depends linearly on \(\rho_{\text{fluid}}\). Salt water density ~1025 kg/m³ vs fresh ~1000 kg/m³, so \(F_B\) increases by about 2.5%.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Archimedes' principle: \(F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{disp}}\). For fully submerged object, \(V_{\text{disp}} = V_{\text{obj}}\), so \(F_B \propto \rho_{\text{fluid}}\). Higher fluid density = larger buoyant force.
reasoning_steps: Note that for full submersion, \(V_{\text{disp}}\) is constant.
misconceptions: Thinking denser fluid means less buoyancy (opposite).
Believing buoyancy depends only on object, not fluid properties.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: F_B = ρ_fluid g V_disp; denser fluid → larger F_B.

glossary_entry.definition: Upward force equal to weight of displaced fluid.

flashcard.front: Buoyant force in salt water vs fresh water (fully submerged)?
flashcard.back: Greater in salt water because F_B ∝ ρ_fluid.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Buoyant force in salt vs fresh water (same object)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Greater in salt water (higher ρ_fluid → larger F_B).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Upward force = weight of displaced fluid.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demo: egg floats in salt water but sinks in fresh water.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. F_B = ρ_fluid g V_disp; higher density gives larger buoyant force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A liquid has a density of \(820\,\text{kg/m}^3\). What is its specific gravity?
questions.hint: SG = ρ_liquid / ρ_water, with ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³.
questions.explanation: Specific gravity = \(820 / 1000 = 0.82\). A value less than 1 means the liquid floats on water. Option A is off by factor 10; Option C is 10× too large; Option D is the density itself.

explanations_tiered.definition: Specific gravity (relative density) is the ratio of a substance's density to the density of water.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since water density is 1000 kg/m³, SG = density (kg/m³) / 1000. SG is dimensionless and <1 indicates floating in water.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a liquid has SG = 0.82, it's 82% as heavy as water — so it will float on water like oil does.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Compute \(SG = \frac{820}{1000} = 0.82\). This is less than 1, so the liquid is less dense than water.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The specific gravity of a substance is \(\frac{\rho}{\rho_{\text{water}}}\), where \(\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\) at 4°C. Thus SG = 0.82, dimensionless.
misconceptions: Specific gravity has units (it does not).
SG >1 means substance floats (it sinks).

glossary_entry.definition: Ratio of substance density to water density.

flashcard.front: SG of liquid with density 820 kg/m³?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: SG is numerically same as density in g/cm³.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What happens to the gauge pressure at a fixed depth if the density of the liquid doubles?
questions.explanation: Gauge pressure at depth h is \(P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h\). If density doubles, pressure doubles proportionally. Options A, C, D do not match the direct proportionality.

explanations_tiered.definition: Gauge pressure is the additional pressure due to the fluid's weight: \(P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At fixed depth, gauge pressure is directly proportional to fluid density. Doubling density doubles pressure.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a column of water: if you use a heavier liquid (like mercury instead of water), the same depth pushes down much harder.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h\), with \(g\) and \(h\) constant, \(P \propto \rho\). So if \(\rho\) doubles, \(P\) doubles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydrostatic equation: \(dP/dh = \rho g\). Integrating, \(P_{\text{gauge}} = \rho g h\) (for constant \(\rho\)). Linear dependence on \(\rho\).
misconceptions: Thinking depth affects density (for incompressible fluids, it doesn't significantly).
Believing pressure depends on container shape (it doesn't; only depth and density matter for gauge pressure).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: P_gauge = ρgh; P ∝ ρ at fixed h.

glossary_entry.definition: Pressure due to fluid weight above a point: ρgh.

flashcard.front: If liquid density doubles, what happens to gauge pressure at same depth?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Density doubles at same depth: gauge pressure?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: ρgh, pressure due to fluid weight.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask: what if depth doubles instead of density?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how pressure depends on depth of fluid
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. P_gauge = ρgh; ρ doubles → P doubles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A fixed amount of ideal gas doubles its absolute temperature while keeping pressure constant. What happens to the volume?
questions.hint: Charles's law: V ∝ T at constant P.
questions.explanation: For an ideal gas at constant pressure, \(V/T = \text{constant}\). Doubling T requires doubling V to maintain constant pressure. Option A is the opposite; Option C would require T constant.

source_trace.content_anchor: Charles's law: V ∝ T at constant P

explanations_tiered.definition: Charles's law states that at constant pressure, the volume of a fixed amount of ideal gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From \(V/T = \text{constant}\), if T doubles, V must double to keep the ratio constant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heat a balloon (but keep pressure the same — let it expand). The balloon gets bigger as the air inside gets hotter.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2\), if \(T_2 = 2T_1\), then \(V_2 = V_1 \times (T_2/T_1) = 2V_1\). Volume doubles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From ideal gas law \(PV = nRT\), with \(P, n, R\) constant, \(V = (nR/P)T\). Thus \(V \propto T\). Doubling T doubles V.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recognize constant P process → Charles's law: \(V \propto T\).
Step 2: If T doubles, V doubles.
reasoning_steps: Recall ideal gas law: PV = nRT.
misconceptions: Using Celsius instead of kelvin (would give wrong ratio).
Applying Boyle's law (constant T) instead of Charles's law.

glossary_entry.definition: At constant pressure, volume of gas is proportional to absolute temperature.

flashcard.front: Constant P, T doubles; what happens to V?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a syringe and hot water.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to pressure, density and specific gravity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. V/T constant → T doubles → V doubles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why does a mercury barometer have a column height of about 0.76 m at sea level, while a water barometer would need about 10.3 m?

options.A: Water is less dense than mercury, so a taller column is needed to balance the same atmospheric pressure
options.B: Mercury expands more with temperature, making the column shorter
options.C: Water evaporates, so the barometer cannot work with water
options.D: Atmospheric pressure is different for water and mercury

questions.hint: Patm = ρgh; for same Patm, h is inversely proportional to ρ.
questions.explanation: Atmospheric pressure is \(P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h\). Mercury density ~13,600 kg/m³, water ~1000 kg/m³. Since ρ_water < ρ_Hg, h_water must be larger (about 13.6×) to produce same pressure. Option B is incorrect; Option C is false (water barometers exist but are impractical).

source_trace.content_anchor: if water instead of mercury were used, a water column of about 10.3 m would be needed

explanations_tiered.definition: A barometer uses a fluid column to balance atmospheric pressure: \(P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For the same Patm, h ∝ 1/ρ. Mercury is 13.6× denser than water, so the mercury column is 13.6× shorter.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mercury is much heavier than water. You only need a short mercury column to balance air pressure, but you'd need a very tall water column — like a three-story building!
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h\), with Patm and g constant, \(h \propto 1/\rho\). ρ_Hg / ρ_water ≈ 13.6, so h_water ≈ 13.6 × h_Hg ≈ 13.6 × 0.76 = 10.3 m.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The barometric equation: \(h = P_{\text{atm}}/(\rho g)\). Since ρ_water << ρ_Hg, h_water >> h_Hg. This makes water barometers impractical.
worked_solution: Step 3: Mercury density 13.6× water → h_water = 13.6 × h_Hg ≈ 10.3 m.
reasoning_steps: Note inverse proportionality between h and ρ.
Explain why water requires taller column.
misconceptions: Thinking water cannot be used in a barometer (it can, but impractically tall).
Believing barometer height depends on tube diameter (it does not).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Patm = ρgh; denser fluid → shorter column.

glossary_entry.definition: Device measuring atmospheric pressure via fluid column height.

flashcard.front: Why is mercury barometer shorter than water barometer?
flashcard.back: Mercury is denser; h ∝ 1/ρ for same Patm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Water barometer taller than mercury? Why?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Water is less dense; h = Patm/(ρg) ∝ 1/ρ.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the working principles of pressure measuring devices like barometers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Patm = ρgh → h ∝ 1/ρ; less dense water needs taller column.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A pipe with cross-sectional area \(0.02\,\text{m}^2\) carries water at \(3.0\,\text{m/s}\). What volume of water flows through in 10 seconds?
questions.hint: First find flow rate Q = Av, then volume = Q × t.
questions.explanation: Flow rate \(Q = A v = 0.02 \times 3.0 = 0.06\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}\). In 10 s, volume = \(0.06 \times 10 = 0.6\,\text{m}^3\). Option A is Q, not volume; Option C is 100× too large.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume flow rate is volume per time: \(Q = Av\). Total volume = \(Q \times t\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First compute Q from area and speed, then multiply by time to get total volume.
explanations_tiered.beginner: How much water comes out of a hose in 10 seconds? First find how much comes out each second (Q), then multiply by 10.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Volumetric flow rate \(Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = Av\). For constant Q, integrated volume \(V = Q \Delta t\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Multiply by time: \(V = Q t = 0.06 \times 10 = 0.6\,\text{m}^3\).
reasoning_steps: Multiply Q by time to get volume.
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply by time (stopping at Q).
Using diameter instead of area in Q = Av.

glossary_entry.definition: Volume of fluid passing per unit time (Q = Av).

flashcard.front: A=0.02 m², v=3.0 m/s, t=10 s. Volume?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A=0.02 m², v=3 m/s, 10 s: volume?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to filling a bucket of known volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An incompressible fluid flows through a pipe that widens from diameter \(d\) to \(2d\). How does the flow speed change?
questions.explanation: Area ratio: \(A_2/A_1 = (2d/d)^2 = 4\). Continuity: \(v_2 = v_1 \times (A_1/A_2) = v_1 / 4\). Speed becomes one-quarter. Option C would be for diameter doubling (area ×4, speed ×1/4, not 1/2).

explanations_tiered.definition: Continuity equation: \(A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\) for incompressible flow. Area is proportional to diameter squared.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When diameter doubles, area quadruples. To maintain constant flow rate, speed must decrease to one-quarter.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a river widens to twice as wide (and deeper too), the water slows down a lot — actually to 1/4 speed if both width and depth double.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Area ∝ d², so \(A_2/A_1 = (2d/d)^2 = 4\). From continuity: \(v_2 = v_1 (A_1/A_2) = v_1/4\). Speed becomes one-quarter.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From mass conservation: \(\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2\). For constant \(\rho\), \(v \propto 1/A \propto 1/d^2\). Doubling d reduces v by factor 4.
misconceptions: Thinking speed halves when diameter doubles (that would be if area doubled, but area quadruples).
Applying linear ratio instead of square ratio.

flashcard.front: Pipe diameter doubles, incompressible. Speed change?

visual_system.visual_description: Pipe narrow left, wide right; diameter ratio 1:2; area ratio 1:4; speed ratio 4:1.
visual_system.caption: Doubling diameter quadruples area, reducing speed to one-quarter.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Pipe diagram: left section narrow (d), right section wide (2d); speed v left, v/4 right.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize area scales with d², not d.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equation of continuity from flow rate
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. A ∝ d²; doubling d gives A₂=4A₁; continuity gives v₂ = v₁/4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of wood with density \(600\,\text{kg/m}^3\) floats in a liquid of density \(800\,\text{kg/m}^3\). What fraction of the block's volume is submerged?
questions.hint: Fraction submerged = ρ_object / ρ_fluid for floating objects.
questions.explanation: Floating condition: \(V_{\text{sub}}/V_{\text{obj}} = \rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = 600/800 = 0.75 = 75\%\). Option A would be 200/800; Option B would be 480/800; Option D >1 impossible.

explanations_tiered.definition: For a floating object, the submerged volume fraction equals the density ratio of object to fluid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From equilibrium: weight = buoyant force → ρ_obj V_obj g = ρ_fluid V_sub g → V_sub/V_obj = ρ_obj/ρ_fluid.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the block is 75% as dense as the liquid, 75% of it will be underwater. It floats high if it's much less dense.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Fraction submerged = ρ_object / ρ_fluid = 600/800 = 0.75 = 75%. So three-quarters of the block is below the surface.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Archimedes: \(F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} V_{\text{sub}} g = W = \rho_{\text{obj}} V_{\text{obj}} g\). Cancelling g: \(V_{\text{sub}}/V_{\text{obj}} = \rho_{\text{obj}}/\rho_{\text{fluid}} = 0.75\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write equilibrium: ρ_obj V_obj g = ρ_fluid V_sub g.
Step 2: Cancel g: ρ_obj V_obj = ρ_fluid V_sub.
reasoning_steps: Divide both sides to isolate submerged fraction.
misconceptions: Using ρ_fluid/ρ_obj (would give >1, impossible).
Forgetting that fraction submerged must be ≤1.

glossary_entry.definition: Equilibrium where weight equals buoyant force, partially submerged.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: If block were denser than fluid, it would sink.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify floating and submerging objects comparing densities

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An open manometer has the gas side higher than the open side by \(0.12\,\text{m}\) of a liquid with density \(900\,\text{kg/m}^3\). Atmospheric pressure is \(101\,\text{kPa}\). What is the absolute gas pressure?
questions.hint: Gas side higher means \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} - \rho g h\).
questions.explanation: Gas side higher indicates lower pressure: \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} - \rho g h = 101\,\text{kPa} - (900 \times 9.8 \times 0.12)/1000 = 101 - 1.0584 = 99.94\,\text{kPa} \approx 99.9\,\text{kPa}\). Option C adds instead of subtracts; Option B ignores the column.

source_trace.content_anchor: P_gas = P_atm - ρgh for gas side higher

explanations_tiered.definition: An open manometer measures pressure difference: \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} \pm \rho g h\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the gas side is higher, gas pressure is less than atmospheric by ρgh.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The liquid is pushed up on the gas side because the gas isn't pushing as hard as the air. So gas pressure = air pressure minus the liquid's extra push.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Gas side higher → P_gas < P_atm. Compute ρgh = 900×9.8×0.12 = 1058 Pa = 1.06 kPa. Then P_gas = 101.0 − 1.06 = 99.9 kPa.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydrostatic equilibrium: P_gas + ρgh (gas side higher) = P_atm, so P_gas = P_atm − ρgh.
worked_solution: Step 1: Gas side higher → subtract ρgh.
reasoning_steps: Identify that gas side higher means P_gas < P_atm.
misconceptions: Always adding ρgh regardless of which side is higher.
Forgetting to convert Pa to kPa.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Gas side higher → P_gas = Patm − ρgh.

glossary_entry.definition: U-tube device measuring gas pressure relative to atmosphere.

flashcard.front: Gas side higher, h=0.12 m, ρ=900, Patm=101 kPa. P_gas?

visual_system.visual_description: U-tube; gas connected arm has higher fluid level than open arm; height difference h.
visual_system.caption: Gas side higher indicates gas pressure is less than atmospheric by ρgh.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: U-tube with left arm (gas) higher fluid level than right arm (open).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Gas side higher manometer: h=0.12 m, ρ=900, Patm=101 kPa. P_gas?
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Gas side higher: P_gas = Patm − ρgh.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with previous manometer problem (gas side lower).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the working principles of pressure measuring devices like manometers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. ρgh = 1.058 kPa; P_gas = 101.0 − 1.058 = 99.9 kPa.
